topic_title: Identifying which components in your system need a mandatory human checkpoint

citation_density.assertion_citation_coverage_estimate: ~72% of standalone factual assertions in sections 5/6/7 carry a citation marker — above the 60% threshold
citation_density.notes: All three [N] markers resolve to resources declared in topic.resources.json. No fabricated citations.

sequence_drift.notes: All technical terms used in the corpus either (a) are introduced by this topic itself (component, risk-tiered component mapping, mandatory checkpoint, error budget, baseline/triggered oversight assignment, blocking/logged/empowered/monitored checkpoint properties) or (b) appear in the allowed prior-concept list (HITL/HOTL/HOOTL from 10.5; automation complacency/accuracy paradox/vigilance decay/trust calibration from 10.4; override-as-theater from 10.1; diffuse accountability from 10.3; Judgment Framework Q1/Q2/Q3 from 9.7–9.9; acceptable error/failure threshold from 9.10; automation bias from 9.5; EU AI Act risk tiers from earlier weeks).

length_sanity.notes: Estimated ~3810 words; consistent with manifest depth_profile_signals estimate of ~3600. Comfortably within the standard corpus range of 3000–4400.

topic_scope.notes: All material stays within the headline scope of 'identifying which components need a mandatory human checkpoint.' Section 5.5 (mandatory vs optional checkpoint properties) is necessary scaffolding for the identification method. Section 5.7 (acceptable error threshold) is explicitly referenced in LO #6 and in the 7-step method (Step 6). The worked example in section 6 applies the method without introducing out-of-scope concepts. Section 8 best practices reinforce the component-level method without teaching a new adjacent concept. No deferred concept is bolded or given a defining clause beyond a name.

findings.detail: HITL, HOTL, HOOTL are spelled out in the Prerequisites block (section 2) but are used without re-expansion at their first body occurrence in section 5.2 (line 67). For a strict first-use-in-body reading, this is a mild gap. Mitigated by the dedicated Prerequisites section that explicitly lists these terms with their full names and topic pointers.

beginner_readability.notes: No paragraph introduces 3+ new terms simultaneously. The four mandatory-checkpoint properties (blocking, logged, empowered, monitored) are presented in a numbered list (not a paragraph), which does not trigger the jargon-dump rule. Sentences are generally direct, active-voice, and address the learner as 'you'. One isolated sentence in section 7.1 (~30 words) exceeds the target length but does not constitute a systematic academic-register pattern. 1 soft finding total — below the hard-fail threshold of 3 distinct findings.

verdict: PASS — all six checks passed. The corpus is well-structured and suitable to ground downstream artifacts. One soft readability finding: HITL/HOTL/HOOTL are not re-expanded at their first body use in section 5.2 (mitigated by the Prerequisites section). No forward references, no scope creep, no fabricated citations. Citation density ~72% across sections 5/6/7, primary resource [1] cited 9 times. Estimated word count ~3810 is within the standard corpus range (3000–4400). Recommend proceeding to HITL #2 corpus review.
